turns.content: Hey, I've read about the macro crates like proc-macro and built-in macros, but I still don't get why we use things like todo!, warn!, or unreachable!. Is it just for documentation?

Not quite, those aren't just for docs. They are compile-time checks that catch errors before your code ever runs on the target platform. The macro crates like proc-macro and built-in macros let you write these safety markers directly in your Rust source files without needing extra dependencies.
But wait, if it's compile-time, why do I see warnings at runtime? Aren't those supposed to be caught by the compiler?

Exactly, that is a common point of confusion. The macro crates like proc-macro and built-in macros generate code at compile time so you can use cfg-gated placeholders to mark incomplete branches safely. When you leave those markers in place without the necessary condition flags, they trigger warnings or panics at runtime instead of failing silently.

Imagine I'm porting a legacy C project into Rust, where there are many temporary functions that are still being written. How should I mark these incomplete branches so the compiler knows they aren't supposed to run at all?

For those temporary functions you're marking as incomplete, you can use the cfg-gated placeholder macro called todo! which tells the Rust compiler this code is intentionally left out and will panic at runtime if executed. This differs from unreachable! because unreachable! causes a compile-time failure rather than a runtime one, so it's used when you want to guarantee the code never runs at all. Since your legacy C project likely has these incomplete functions scattered throughout, using cfg-gated placeholders with todo! allows them to be compiled without errors while still serving as clear markers that they are not meant to be executed in production.

That makes sense for runtime panics, but what if I want the compiler to warn me about this code path at compile time instead of letting it run? Can I use a different macro or trait bound to catch these incomplete branches earlier?

You can't use a single macro to replace todo! because that's specifically designed for runtime panics, but you can combine macros with attributes to achieve what you want. Instead of just using the plain macro, you would wrap it in a #[allow(dead_code)] or #[cfg(feature = "dev")] attribute so it only compiles during development phases and gets removed from release builds. This way, the compiler won't even try to execute it at runtime because the cfg-gated conditional prevents compilation of that code path entirely when the feature flag is off.

Wait, I thought macros like this were always compiled into the final binary? If I use #[cfg(feature = "dev")] then shouldn't they still be part of the release binary since it's just a build-time check?

No, you are mistaken about how cfg-gated code works because Rust does not include any code that is gated behind false conditions in the final artifact. When you compile with feature flags disabled, the compiler simply removes those entire blocks from the output entirely rather than keeping them as dead code or warnings. This means your incomplete branches marked with cfg-gated placeholders will disappear completely from the release binary, ensuring no one ever accidentally executes them at runtime.
